explanation.pearls: The calcium-to-creatinine clearance ratio (CaCr ratio) below 0.01 suggests familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia rather than primary hyperparathyroidism; a ratio above 0.02 favours PHPT, while 0.01-0.02 is indeterminate and genetic testing for CaSR mutations may be needed.

subtopic_detail: Multiple sclerosis - clinically isolated syndrome workup and management
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 5-day history of progressive blurring and pain on eye movement in her left eye. Visual acuity is 6/36 in the left eye and 6/6 in the right. She has a left relative afferent pupillary defect. Colour vision is impaired in the left eye. Fundoscopy shows a mildly swollen optic disc on the left. The remainder of her neurological examination is normal. She has no prior neurological symptoms.

data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: left optic nerve enhancement with gadolinium, plus 4 periventricular T2/FLAIR white matter lesions (3 ovoid, perpendicular to the corpus callosum), 1 juxtacortical lesion, and 1 infratentorial lesion. MRI spine: no spinal cord lesions. CSF: oligoclonal bands present (absent in serum), mildly elevated protein

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management approach?

options.text: Observation only as she has had a single clinical episode without recurrence
options.rationale: While she has had only one clinical episode (optic neuritis), the MRI demonstrates dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial lesions) and CSF oligoclonal bands. Under the 2017 McDonald criteria, this meets the criteria for MS diagnosis, as CSF OCBs can substitute for dissemination in time. Early disease-modifying therapy is recommended to reduce relapse risk and disability accumulation. (Source: MSIF: McDonald Criteria 2017)

options.text: IV methylprednisolone for acute episode then commence disease-modifying therapy
options.rationale: Acute optic neuritis with significant visual loss should be treated with IV methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3-5 days) to hasten visual recovery. Given the MRI findings meeting 2017 McDonald criteria for MS (dissemination in space on MRI plus CSF OCBs substituting for dissemination in time), early disease-modifying therapy should be commenced to reduce relapse risk and prevent disability accumulation. (Source: MSIF: McDonald Criteria 2017)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg tapering course for optic neuritis treatment
options.rationale: The Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial demonstrated that oral prednisolone alone (without prior IV methylprednisolone) was associated with a higher relapse rate of optic neuritis than placebo. Oral steroids alone are not recommended for acute optic neuritis. IV methylprednisolone is the appropriate acute treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Repeat MRI in 6 months to demonstrate dissemination in time for diagnosis
options.rationale: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow CSF oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time, meaning a second MRI is not required to confirm the diagnosis in this patient. Waiting 6 months delays treatment and increases the risk of further relapses and disability. (Source: MSIF: McDonald Criteria 2017)

options.text: Plasma exchange for the acute optic neuritis with ongoing visual loss
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is reserved for severe relapses (particularly myelitis or brainstem attacks) that fail to respond to IV methylprednisolone. It is not first-line for optic neuritis and should only be considered if IV steroids produce no improvement after 5 days. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: A clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) is a single clinical episode consistent with demyelination. The 2017 McDonald criteria allow MS diagnosis at CIS if MRI demonstrates dissemination in space (lesions in >=2 of 4 typical locations: periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) and dissemination in time (new lesion on follow-up MRI, simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, OR CSF oligoclonal bands). This patient meets criteria for MS at first presentation, allowing early treatment. The Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial established IV methylprednisolone as the acute treatment standard.
key_points: 2017 McDonald criteria allow MS diagnosis at first clinical episode with appropriate MRI and CSF findings
CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for dissemination in time
IV methylprednisolone hastens visual recovery in optic neuritis but does not alter long-term visual outcome
Oral prednisolone alone is contraindicated for optic neuritis due to increased relapse risk
explanation.pearls: Relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) persists after optic neuritis recovery even when visual acuity returns to 6/6, because the damaged optic nerve conducts impulses more slowly; this permanent finding can be the only clinical evidence of a previous episode when evaluating dissemination in time.

subtopic_detail: B12 deficiency with subacute combined degeneration - urgent replacement
stem: A 65-year-old woman with a history of autoimmune gastritis presents with a 3-month history of progressive unsteadiness, tingling in both hands and feet, and difficulty buttoning her shirt. On examination she has reduced vibration and proprioception in the hands and feet, brisk knee reflexes with absent ankle reflexes, bilateral extensor plantar responses, and a wide-based ataxic gait. Romberg test is positive.

data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 112 fL, reticulocyte count 0.8%, blood film: hypersegmented neutrophils and macro-ovalocytes, B12 68 pmol/L (ref >150, severely deficient), folate 22 nmol/L (normal), homocysteine 45 micromol/L (elevated, ref <15), methylmalonic acid 1.8 micromol/L (elevated, ref <0.4), intrinsic factor antibodies positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral cyanocobalamin 1000 microg daily as adequate replacement therapy
options.rationale: While high-dose oral B12 can achieve adequate absorption through passive diffusion even without intrinsic factor, neurological involvement (subacute combined degeneration) is an indication for parenteral replacement. Intramuscular injections ensure rapid and reliable repletion, which is critical when neurological damage is progressing. Oral supplementation may be used for maintenance after neurological recovery. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily with oral B12 supplementation to address anaemia
options.rationale: Folic acid supplementation in the context of B12 deficiency can correct the anaemia but does NOT treat or prevent neurological damage, and may even mask the haematological markers of B12 deficiency while neurological deterioration continues. B12 must be replaced first or simultaneously, and folic acid alone is dangerous in this setting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: IM hydroxocobalamin 1000 microg on alternate days for 2 weeks then maintenance
options.rationale: Subacute combined degeneration with neurological involvement requires urgent parenteral B12 replacement. The standard Australian regimen is IM hydroxocobalamin 1000 microg on alternate days for 2 weeks (to rapidly replete stores and halt neurological progression), then every 3 months indefinitely for pernicious anaemia. Early treatment maximises the chance of neurological recovery, but established damage may be irreversible. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Packed red cell transfusion followed by oral iron and B12 supplementation
options.rationale: The anaemia is megaloblastic due to B12 deficiency and should respond to B12 replacement. Transfusion is not indicated at Hb 98 g/L without haemodynamic compromise. B12 replacement will stimulate erythropoiesis with a reticulocyte peak at 7-10 days. Iron is not indicated as this is not iron deficiency. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Referral for MRI spine before commencing treatment to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: While MRI may show characteristic posterior and lateral column signal changes in subacute combined degeneration, treatment should not be delayed for imaging. The combination of severely low B12, megaloblastic anaemia, positive intrinsic factor antibodies, and the classic neurological triad (peripheral neuropathy, posterior column dysfunction, and pyramidal signs) is diagnostic. Delaying B12 replacement risks irreversible neurological damage. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: B12 Deficiency)

explanation.summary: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord is caused by severe B12 deficiency affecting the posterior columns (loss of vibration and proprioception), lateral corticospinal tracts (upper motor neuron signs), and peripheral nerves (absent ankle reflexes). The paradoxical finding of absent ankle reflexes with brisk knee reflexes and extensor plantars reflects combined peripheral neuropathy and myelopathy. Pernicious anaemia (autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells) is confirmed by positive intrinsic factor antibodies. Urgent parenteral B12 replacement is essential as neurological damage may become irreversible if treatment is delayed.
key_points: Absent ankle reflexes with extensor plantars indicates combined peripheral neuropathy and myelopathy
Neurological involvement mandates parenteral rather than oral B12 replacement
Folic acid alone can mask haematological markers while neurological damage progresses
Intrinsic factor antibodies are highly specific (close to 100%) but only 50-60% sensitive for pernicious anaemia
explanation.pearls: Neurological manifestations of B12 deficiency can occur before anaemia develops or with a normal MCV; up to 28% of patients with B12 deficiency neuropathy have a normal MCV, so a normal blood count does not exclude B12 deficiency as a cause of neurological symptoms.

subtopic_detail: Haemochromatosis - HFE genotyping and venesection initiation
stem: A 45-year-old man of Northern European descent presents with fatigue, arthralgia affecting the 2nd and 3rd metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally, and mildly abnormal liver function tests found incidentally. He drinks 6 standard drinks per week. On examination he has a mildly enlarged liver but no stigmata of chronic liver disease. His fasting transferrin saturation is markedly elevated on two separate occasions.

data_table.Investigations: Ferritin 1850 microg/L (ref 30-300), transferrin saturation 82% (ref 16-50, confirmed on repeat fasting sample), ALT 62 U/L, AST 55 U/L, GGT 48 U/L, ALP 72 U/L, albumin 38 g/L, Hb 158 g/L, platelets 195 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Liver biopsy with Perls staining to quantify hepatic iron concentration
options.rationale: Liver biopsy was previously the gold standard but has been largely replaced by HFE genotyping for diagnosis and MRI for iron quantification. Biopsy is now reserved for staging fibrosis when ferritin exceeds 1000 microg/L in confirmed haemochromatosis, not as the initial diagnostic test. (Source: GESA: Haemochromatosis Guidelines)

options.text: MRI liver with T2* quantification to measure hepatic iron content
options.rationale: MRI T2* (FerriScan) is useful for quantifying hepatic iron overload and can detect fibrosis, but it is a staging investigation performed after confirming the genetic diagnosis. The primary diagnostic test for suspected hereditary haemochromatosis is HFE genotyping. (Source: GESA: Haemochromatosis Guidelines)

options.text: Serum hepcidin level to assess iron regulatory hormone function
options.rationale: Hepcidin testing is primarily a research tool and is not routinely available or recommended in clinical practice for haemochromatosis diagnosis. HFE genotyping is the standard diagnostic approach and is widely available and Medicare-rebated. (Source: GESA: Haemochromatosis Guidelines)

options.text: HFE gene mutation analysis for C282Y and H63D polymorphisms
options.rationale: In a Northern European patient with elevated transferrin saturation (>45%) and elevated ferritin, HFE genotyping is the diagnostic test of choice. C282Y homozygosity confirms hereditary haemochromatosis (type 1) and is found in approximately 85% of clinically affected patients in Australia. Compound heterozygosity (C282Y/H63D) accounts for a smaller proportion. Testing is Medicare-rebated when transferrin saturation exceeds 45%. (Source: GESA: Haemochromatosis Guidelines)

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound to assess for hepatocellular carcinoma screening
options.rationale: HCC screening with ultrasound is important in established haemochromatosis with cirrhosis, but it is a surveillance tool rather than a diagnostic investigation. The priority is confirming the diagnosis with HFE genotyping, then assessing for end-organ damage including fibrosis staging and HCC risk. (Source: GESA: Haemochromatosis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Hereditary haemochromatosis is the most common genetic condition in people of Northern European descent, with C282Y homozygosity present in approximately 1 in 200 Australians. Iron overload causes end-organ damage including liver fibrosis/cirrhosis, diabetes, cardiomyopathy, arthropathy, and hypogonadism. The diagnostic pathway is: elevated fasting transferrin saturation (>45%), HFE genotyping, then staging (ferritin level, liver fibrosis assessment). Venesection to maintain ferritin below 50-100 microg/L is the standard treatment and prevents further organ damage. The characteristic arthropathy affecting the 2nd and 3rd MCPs with hook-shaped osteophytes is a clinical clue.
key_points: Fasting transferrin saturation >45% on two occasions is the screening threshold for HFE genotyping
C282Y homozygosity confirms hereditary haemochromatosis in 85% of clinically affected Australians
Ferritin >1000 microg/L warrants assessment for hepatic fibrosis (MRI or biopsy)
Venesection targets ferritin below 50-100 microg/L and prevents further end-organ damage
explanation.pearls: The 2nd and 3rd MCP arthropathy of haemochromatosis is caused by calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition (pseudogout) triggered by iron-mediated chondrocyte damage; X-ray characteristically shows hook-shaped osteophytes and chondrocalcinosis, and the arthropathy may not improve with venesection as the joint damage is structural.
